original_abstract: This study utilizes pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR spectroscopy to investigate the self-diffusion of molecular and ionic components in aqueous mixtures of choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents (DESs). By examining Ethaline, Glyceline, and Reline, the research clarifies how varying water concentrations influence both macroscopic transport properties and microscopic intermolecular interactions. The results demonstrate that the addition of water significantly alters the diffusion behavior of hydroxyl protons. In aqueous Ethaline, the hydroxyl species of both the choline cation and the hydrogen bond donor (HBD) exhibit enhanced interactions with water as hydration increases. In contrast, Glyceline maintains relatively stable hydroxyl proton dynamics despite water addition. Notably, in Reline, the presence of water appears to facilitate the localized formation of ammonium hydroxide. A significant finding across the systems is that water often diffuses faster than predicted for a homogeneous mixture, suggesting that these liquids undergo microscopic phase separation even when components are nominally hydrophilic. These insights underscore the utility of PFG NMR in deconstructing the complex competitive interactions and structural inhomogeneities that define the behavior of aqueous DES systems.
humanized_abstract: The self-diffusion of the molecular and ionic constituents in aqueous solutions of choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents (DESs), namely Ethaline, Glyceline and Reline was investigated by means of pulsed field gradient (PFG) 1H NMR spectroscopy in a wide range of water concentrations, hence allowing to gain insights on the relationship between the macroscopic transport properties and the intermolecular interactions established at the molecular level by changing the water composition. It is shown that by adding water into the DESs the diffusion behavior of the hydroxyl protons strongly changes. While in aqueous Ethaline solutions, choline hydroxyls and HBD hydroxyls interact with water to a larger extent with increasing the water content, in aqueous Glyceline solutions the proton dynamics does not change substantially upon the addition of water. In aqueous Reline solutions, water appears to enhance the formation of ammonium hydroxide. Most importantly, in all the DESs here investigated lead to water diffusing in a fashion that is higher than what expected for a homogeneous solution. This further supports the concept that all DESs lead to microscopic phase-separation, at least at the level of the diffusive motions investigated by the PFG NMR experiments, even for hydrophilic constituents and hence opens a new, although, not unexpected perspective on their structure and behavior.

It is shown that by adding water into the DESs the diffusion behavior of the hydroxyl protons strongly changes. While in aqueous Ethaline solutions, choline hydroxyls and HBD hydroxyls interact with water to a larger extent with increasing the water content, in aqueous Glyceline solutions the proton dynamics does not change substantially upon the addition of water. In aqueous Reline solutions, water appears to enhance the formation of ammonium hydroxide. Most importantly, in all the DESs here investigated lead to water diffusing in a fashion that is higher than what expected for a homogeneous solution. This further supports the concept that all DESs lead to microscopic phase-separation, at least at the level of the diffusive motions investigated by the PFG NMR experiments, even for hydrophilic constituents and hence opens a new, although, not unexpected perspective on their structure and behavior.
